title: Executive Order 11992—Committee on Selection of Federal Judicial Officers

flags.justification: A purely advisory, investigative committee reporting candidate names to the President, who retains full appointment discretion; Sec. 4 makes Committee members ineligible for the very positions they vet, a real conflict-of-interest safeguard.
Sec. 3(c) requires a report within 60 days listing no more than five candidates — a specific, checkable deadline and format.
Single coherent purpose: vetting candidates for a narrow category of judicial appointments.

The Committee applies 'criteria established by the President or by the Attorney General,' deferring the actual substantive selection criteria to a separate, unspecified process rather than defining them here.
Sec. 4's ineligibility rule is a positive design feature preventing an obvious self-dealing conflict of interest.

Sec. 8 sets an explicit termination date: 'shall terminate on December 31, 1978, unless sooner extended' — a clean default toward termination requiring affirmative extension rather than affirmative termination.

Sec. 7 delegates most Federal Advisory Committee Act compliance functions to the Attorney General but explicitly excludes the annual congressional reporting requirement from that delegation — a technical carve-out preserving the reporting chain, not a removal of it.
The Federal Advisory Committee Act's own transparency requirements govern this Committee's operations, combined with the member-ineligibility safeguard.
The order's own text is narrowly scoped, explicitly excluding the most significant category of judicial appointments (circuit and district judges); it claims no broad or reusable authority.
The Attorney General provides administrative support by design, not a competing mandate.
No coverage population or exemption structure is created.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 1/18 = 5.6%. First Carter-era order in this corpus, narrowly scoped to non-Article-III judicial appointments.
